questions.question: Explain the difference between function templates and class templates with an example of each.
keyPoints: Function templates define generic functions that work with any type - example: template<typename T> T max(T a, T b)
Class templates define generic classes - example: template<typename T> class Stack { }
Function templates invoked directly, class templates require instantiation with type

questions.question: What is template specialization? Differentiate between full specialization and partial specialization.
keyPoints: Template specialization provides custom implementation for specific types
Full specialization: specialize all template parameters - template<> class Stack<int> { }
Partial specialization: specialize only some parameters - template<typename T> class Stack<T*> { }
Only class templates support partial specialization

questions.question: Write a C++ program to demonstrate the use of std::vector with integers and strings.
keyPoints: Include <vector> header
Create vector<int> and vector<string>
Use push_back to add elements
Use size(), at(), iterators to access elements
Show example output demonstrating both types

questions.question: Explain the concept of iterators in STL. How are they different from pointers?
keyPoints: Iterators provide unified access to container elements
Acts like generalized pointers but work with container internals
Categories: input, output, forward, bidirectional, random access
Different from raw pointers: not memory addresses, controlled by container, can be invalidated

questions.question: What are the advantages of using templates in C++?
keyPoints: Code reusability - write once, use with any type
Type safety - compile-time type checking
Performance - no runtime overhead compared to void*
Generic programming - algorithm works with any container

questions.question: Describe the differences between std::map and std::unordered_map containers.
keyPoints: std::map: ordered, implemented as red-black tree, O(log n) operations
std::unordered_map: unordered, hash table, O(1) average operations
map supports range-based operations, maintains sorted order
unordered_map may have worse worst-case performance

questions.question: What is SFINAE? How is it useful in template programming?
expactedPoints: SFINAE: Substitution Failure Is Not An Error
During overload resolution, if template substitution fails, that overload is silently discarded
Used to enable/disable function templates based on type traits
Enables compile-time polymorphism without virtual functions

questions.question: Write a complete C++ program to implement a generic Stack class using templates that supports push, pop, top, and isEmpty operations. Demonstrate its use with int and double types.
keyPoints: Define template<class T> class Stack
Include private: vector<T> elements data member
Public methods: push(const T&), pop(), top(), isEmpty()
Include necessary exception handling for empty stack
Main function: create Stack<int> and Stack<double>, demonstrate all operations
Show sample output

questions.question: Explain STL containers, algorithms, and iterators with examples of at least two containers and two algorithms each.
keyPoints: Containers: sequence (vector, list, deque), associative (map, set), adapters (stack, queue)
Examples with vector: push_back, size, access operations
Examples with map: insert, find, iteration
Algorithms: sorting (sort), searching (find), transformation (transform)
Iterators: begin(), end(), increment, dereference

questions.question: Write a C++ program to create a vector of integers, perform sorting, and use std::find to search for an element. Display the results.
keyPoints: Include <vector>, <algorithm>, <iostream>
Create vector<int> with sample values
Use std::sort to sort the vector
Use std::find to search for a value
Display sorted vector and search result (found/not found)
Use iterators for traversal

questions.question: Discuss the concept of non-type template parameters and variadic templates in C++. Provide examples to illustrate their usage.
keyPoints: Non-type parameters: accept constant values as template arguments - template<int N> class Array { T arr[N] }
Variadic templates: accept variable number of arguments - template<typename... Args>
Pack expansion: Args... expands to individual types
Practical uses: std::tuple, printf-style formatting, type lists
